title: Choosing the Right AWS Compute Model
description:
## The AWS Compute Spectrum

There is no single "best" compute service on AWS. The right choice is a trade-off between **how much control you need**, **how much operational burden you are willing to carry**, and **how the workload scales** (including whether it can scale to zero). As a Solutions Architect you should be able to defend a choice along that spectrum.

### The four broad options

| Service | Abstraction | You manage | AWS manages | Scale-to-zero? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EC2** | Virtual machines | OS, patching, capacity, scaling logic | Hypervisor, hardware | No (instances always cost while running) |
| **ECS / EKS on EC2** | Container orchestration on your instances | Container instances (the data plane), scaling | Orchestration plumbing (EKS control plane is managed) | No |
| **Fargate** | Serverless containers | Container image, task/pod definition | Servers, patching, capacity | Effectively yes (scale tasks down) |
| **Lambda** | Event-driven functions | Function code + config | Everything else (runtime, scaling, capacity) | **Yes** (zero cost when idle) |

### How to reason about the choice

> **More control ⇄ more operational burden.** Moving down the table trades flexibility for less undifferentiated heavy lifting.

- **EC2** — pick when you need full control of the OS/kernel, specialized instance types (GPU, bare metal), licensing tied to a host, or lift-and-shift of a legacy app.
- **ECS on EC2** — containerized workloads where you want to manage the underlying fleet (e.g. to use Spot, GPUs, or pack many small tasks densely).
- **ECS on Fargate** — containers without managing any servers or a Kubernetes control plane; lowest ops for container workloads.
- **EKS** — you specifically need the Kubernetes API/ecosystem (Helm, operators, portability across clouds). More operational surface than ECS.
- **Lambda** — event-driven, spiky, or unpredictable workloads; pay only per invocation/duration; ideal when idle periods are common. Watch the 15-minute max duration and cold starts.

### Rule of thumb

Start at the **most managed** option that meets the requirement and only move toward EC2 when a hard constraint (kernel access, long-running process, specialized hardware, license) forces you to. This minimizes the operational tax over the life of the workload.
